Figma Gains on Strong Growth Outlook That Eases AI Fears
data.description:
Figma (FIG) gave an annual revenue outlook that topped estimates, easing Wall Street anxiety that the business is threatened by the emergence of rival artificial intelligence products. Sales will be about $1.37 billion in 2026, the San Francisco-based company said Wednesday in a statement. Analysts, on average, projected $1.29 billion, according to data compiled by Bloomberg. Figma is known for making software used to design applications and website interfaces. It has worked to expand its suite of software, including a generative AI tool called Figma Make, which creates app interfaces and code from prompts. Weekly users of the tool grew 70% in the fourth quarter, the company said in the statement. Figma CEO Dylan Field joins Bloomberg Businessweek Daily to discuss. He speaks with Carol Massar and Tim Stenovec.

Bloomberg videos, provided by MT Newswires

European Midday Briefing : Luxury Stocks Drive Indexes Gains -2-
data.description:
Trump appointees in charge of the Justice Department said that after an "exhaustive review" they had found no incriminating client list. "We did not uncover evidence that could predicate an investigation against uncharged third parties," the Justice Department and FBI wrote in a memo released in July.

As Peace Talks Stall, Russia and Ukraine Share One Aim: Keep Trump Happy

GENEVA-In talks about ending the war, Ukraine and Russia have converged on one goal: to avoid angering President Trump by appearing to block peace.

They continue to send delegations to talks mediated by U.S. officials who applaud their constructive attitude and report back to Trump, who seeks a peace deal in Ukraine as his greatest diplomatic victory.

data.title: European Midday Briefing : Luxury Stocks Drive Indexes Gains
data.description:
MARKET WRAPS

Stocks:

European shares were on the rise on Friday, as luxury stocks supported gains across indexes.

Moncler surged more than 12% after reporting revenue ahead of estimates, driving the FTSE MIB up. LVMH also rose almost 3%, spurring the CAC 40 higher.

Oil prices were heading for a weekly gain, while gold remained above the $5,000 mark as investors braced for a possible U.S. strike on Iran .

"For now, markets are still trying to gauge how much of it is just posturing to strengthen the U.S. negotiation positions," ING said.

"The key question is then how prolonged any action would be and what the ultimate aim of the U.S. is." it added.

Investors' attention is now turning to the U.S. PCE inflation data , the Federal Reserve's preferred inflation measure, and advance fourth-quarter economic growth data later today.

Weak data could reignite rate-cut expectations , though recent indicators have pointed to a robust economy.

Economic insight

The U.K. government had a record budget surplus in January . The data support other evidence that the U.K. economy started the year looking much healthier, giving Treasury chief Rachel Reeves something positive to point to in her March 3 fiscal statement, Capital Economics said.

The latest German purchasing managers' survey came in stronger than expected . It follows a slightly better-than-expected French composite print for February.

Private-sector activity in the euro area expanded in February at a stronger pace than anticipated, signaling that recovery across the bloc is gaining momentum despite lingering headwinds.

U.S. Markets:

Stock futures were up on Friday as investors attention turned to data and U.S.-Iran tensions.

PCE inflation data and advance fourth quarter economic growth data are both due at 1330 GMT. The flash S&P purchasing managers' survey will also be released at 1445 GMT.

Forex:

The euro fell, showing limited reaction even after the eurozone's purchasing managers' index survey exceeded forecasts.

Sterling rose against the euro after U.K. data Friday showed retail sales rose more than expected in January and the government recorded a record budget surplus in January.

The dollar traded steady against a basket of currencies ahead of U.S. data.

The Swedish krona rose after inflation data for January were revised higher , supporting expectations that the Riksbank was likely finished cutting interest rates.

Bonds:

Eurozone bond yields marginally trimmed losses.

Yields on German government bonds fell . ING analysts said they could be favored over Treasurys amid worries about a possible U.S. strike on Iran. "Bunds might be preferred over Treasurys to hide from Iran-related turmoil," it said.

Treasury yields stabilized after rising on Thursday. The rise came after Fed minutes showed policymakers had little appetite to cut interest rates.

Treasurys were caught between risk-averse sentiment prompting safe-haven flows and concerns about the potential inflationary consequences of higher oil prices, Deutsche Bank said.

Energy:

Oil prices were on track for weekly gains of more than 5%, buoyed by rising U.S.-Iran fears.

Gas

European natural-gas prices extended gains in early trading, driven by fears of potential supply disruptions in the Strait of Hormuz--a key waterway for LNG transit.

Metals:

Gold prices held above $5,000, as a U.S. military buildup in the Middle East and fears of a potential strike against Iran lifted demand for safe-haven assets.

"Prices have been volatile since a sharp correction from January's record highs, driven partly by speculative trading, but underlying support factors, including geopolitical risks and a shift away from traditional assets , remain in place," MUFG said.

Gold chart

Comex gold futures' bullish momentum remained in play, as the relative strength index was above the 50% threshold on a daily chart, RHB Retail Research said.

Base metals

Base metals were broadly higher in Asian trading hours.

The complex appears to be stabilizing rather than trending , with rallies meeting resistance and volumes clustering near turning points, Sucden Financial said.

EMEA HEADLINES

Eurozone Business Activity Picks Up Faster Than Expected

Private-sector activity in the euro area expanded at a stronger pace than anticipated in February, driven by a rebound in industry as the bloc's economy continues to prove resilient against lingering headwinds.

Data firm S&P Global said Friday that its measure of activity at manufacturers and service providers in the eurozone rose to 51.9 in February, compared with 51.3 a month prior. A consensus of economists polled by The Wall Street Journal expected a reading of 51.5.

Christine Lagarde Says Her 'Baseline' Is Finishing ECB Term

Christine Lagarde said she expects completing her mission as president of the European Central Bank will take until the end of her term, amid speculation that she will resign early to allow the French government to help pick her successor before next year's election.

"When I look back at all these years, I think that we have accomplished a lot, that I have accomplished a lot," she said in an interview with The Wall Street Journal on Thursday. "We need to consolidate and make sure that this is really solid and reliable. So my baseline is that it will take until the end of my term."

Anglo American Halves Value of De Beers Diamond Business

Anglo American wrote down the value of its De Beers diamond business by $2.3 billion, the third cut in three years, as challenging market conditions complicate plans to dispose of the unit.

The London-listed miner warned earlier this month that it was undertaking a review of the business that could lead to the write-down. For 2025, the unit booked a roughly $500 million underlying earnings loss.

Air Liquide Posts Higher Profit on Sales Growth in Gas, Services Business

Air Liquide posted a rise in full-year net profit, supported by strong momentum and higher revenue in its gas and services businesses.

The French industrial-gases company said Friday that net profit for 2025 rose by 6.4% on a reported basis to 3.52 billion euros ($4.14 billion), while revenue increased by 2% on a comparable basis to 26.94 billion euros.

GLOBAL NEWS

New Inflation Data Could Send a 'Pause' Signal to the Fed

The Federal Reserve's preferred inflation gauge is expected to show prices rose faster in December, reinforcing policymakers' increasing caution on cutting interest rates.

The consensus call among economists surveyed by FactSet is that core personal consumption expenditures, which don't include volatile food or energy prices, rose 0.37% in December from the prior month and 3.0% from a year earlier, up from 2.8% in November. Headline PCE is forecast to increase 0.3% on the month and 2.7% year over year.

The S&P 500 is undergoing a historic shift that could reshape the stock market

For the past few years, most of the gains seen by major U.S. equity indexes such as the S&P 500 and the Nasdaq composite were driven by a handful of megacap stocks. But recently, that dynamic has started to change.

As a result, the benchmark S&P 500 SPX, the market-cap-weighted index that's defined the U.S. stock market for generations of investors, is underperforming its equal-weighted sibling XX:SP500EW by the widest margin at this point in the year since the early 1990s.

Here's what's driving the U.S. dollar to its strongest position in almost a month - and it's not just Iran tensions

The dollar reached its strongest position of the past month on Thursday, helped by support from investors that boiled down to more than just one simple catalyst.

The ICE U.S. Dollar Index DXY, a popular gauge of the greenback versus a basket of six major peers, touched a session high of 98.07 on Thursday before slipping to almost 97.9 - levels which have not been seen since Jan. 22. Reasons for the rally included a potential U.S. military strike against Iran within a matter of days, economic data that showed surprising strength in some parts of the economy, and recent turmoil in the U.S. stock market that boosted the safe-haven appeal of the U.S. currency.

Trump Weighs Initial Limited Strike to Force Iran Into Nuclear Deal

WASHINGTON-President Trump is weighing an initial limited military strike on Iran to force it to meet his demands for a nuclear deal, a first step that would be designed to pressure Tehran into an agreement but fall short of a full-scale attack that could inspire a major retaliation.

The opening assault, which if authorized could come within days, would target a few military or government sites, people familiar with the matter said. If Iran still refused to comply with Trump's directive to end its nuclear enrichment, the U.S. would respond with a broad campaign against regime facilities-potentially aimed at toppling the Tehran regime.

A Confident Kim Embarks on New Era of Defiance at North Korea Conclave

SEOUL-North Korean dictator Kim Jong Un kicked off his country's most important political event in half a decade with confidence, gearing up for an era of military expansion and defiance as a self-declared nuclear power.

The 9th Party Congress, where North Korea's leadership sets out its foreign policy and economic and military goals for the next five years, opened Thursday with a celebratory atmosphere. During the last congress, in 2021, a somber Kim admitted to economic failures. But this year, Kim said he was filled with optimism.

Europe Is Finding Plenty to Investigate in the Epstein Files

When the Justice Department decided last year not to release its Epstein files, U.S. officials said there was nothing in the documents that warranted further prosecution.

(MORE TO FOLLOW) Dow Jones Newswires

02-20-26 0517ET

Danone Forecasts Steady Sales Growth, Limited Hit From Infant-Formula Recalls -- Update
data.description:
By Aimee Look


Danone projected steady sales growth for the year ahead, with a muted impact from infant-formula recalls, as the French food maker sought to reassure investors that the hit wouldn't linger for long.

The company behind Aptamil baby milk has been one of the companies affected by a tainted ingredient in its infant formula. Over the past few months, food companies including Danone and Nestle issued warnings or pulled infant nutrition labels off the shelf in more than 60 countries. Danone's stock has fallen this year amid the recalls.

Danone, also home to brands like Activia yogurt and Evian water, on Friday estimated the recalls would shave between 0.5% and 1% off the group's net sales in the first quarter. It forecast like-for-like sales to increase by 3% to 5% for the year as a whole, in line with its midterm target and with its guidance in recent years.

The company said recall processes were under way in coordination with authorities, and executives said in a call with analysts that it was too early to quantify the impact on consumer sentiment in Europe. The company said it would provide a final assessment of the recalls' impact once they are finalized, with the current estimate pointing to an immaterial impact.

Danone's outlook should "reassure investors looking for stability," Barclays analyst Warren Ackerman said in a note to clients.

The update from Danone came after Swiss peer Nestle on Thursday said it anticipated infant-formula recalls to lead to a direct hit to sales growth of 0.9% in the first quarter and 0.2% for the year as a whole, but cautioned that uncertainty about indirect impacts could mean full-year organic sales growth landed at the lower end of its 3% to 4% range.

Danone said momentum, particularly in its high protein business in North America, helped its top line in the fourth quarter, and reported a 4.7% rise in like-for-like sales. Analysts had expected the metric to rise 4.4% for the quarter, according to company-compiled estimates.

For the fourth quarter, volumes grew 2.5%, while prices increased 2.1%, the company said.

On a reported basis, quarterly sales fell to 6.68 billion euros ($7.86 billion) compared with 6.72 billion euros in the same period a year prior. Analysts had expected 6.67 billion euros in sales for the quarter.

Danone made a net profit for the full year of 1.825 billion euros, down from 2.02 billion a year before. The company said it booked a nonrecurring loss of 725 million euros, which included one-off costs related to transformation projects in Europe and Indonesia.

Despite questions on the hit to its infant-formula business, Danone otherwise had a strong showing, analysts said.

Shares traded 0.6% lower in European morning trading.


Write to Aimee Look at aimee.look@wsj.com


(END) Dow Jones Newswires

02-20-26 0509ET

Gold rises on US-Iran tensions and lower European bond yields
data.description:
Feb 20 (Reuters) - Gold prices rose on Friday, supported by lingering tensions between the United States and Iran and lower European bond yields while investors awaited U.S. inflation data to assess the outlook for Federal Reserve monetary policy.

Spot gold was up 0.7% at $5,032.49 an ounce by 0941 GMT, but was on course for a weekly decline of 0.2%. U.S. gold futures for April delivery were up 1.1% at $5,052.70.

"What we're seeing in the interest rate markets is that prices are higher in the longer end in European trading. That means yields are down, and that is reducing opportunity costs for holding gold," said Quantitative Commodity Research analyst Peter Fertig.

 Euro zone government bond yields were set for a second straight weekly decline on Friday in a week marked by speculation over the leadership of the European Central Bank and by rising geopolitical tensions.

U.S. President Donald Trump warned Iran on Thursday that it must make a deal over its nuclear programme or "really bad things" will happen, setting a deadline of 10 to 15 days. 

Goldman Sachs said in a note that in its base case, with central bank buying re-accelerating and private investors adding exposure only in response to U.S. interest rate cuts, it expects gold to grind slowly higher this year.

U.S. personal consumption expenditure data, the Federal Reserve's preferred inflation gauge, is in focus for clues on U.S. monetary policy. Economists polled by Reuters forecast a 0.3% rise in core PCE. The data is due at 1330 GMT.

Traders largely expect the central bank to hold interest rates steady at its March policy meeting, according to CME's FedWatch Tool. [FEDWATCH] 

Non-yielding bullion tends to perform well when interest rates are low.

Gold demand remained subdued in India this week as volatile prices deterred buyers while other major Asian hubs, including China, were closed for the Lunar New Year holidays. [GOL/AS]

Elsewhere, spot silver gained 2.8% to $80.57 an ounce. Spot platinum rose 2.3% to $2,117.66 while palladium added 2.3% to $1,723.25. All metals were poised to register weekly gains. 

(Reporting by Pablo Sinha in Bengaluru)

By Pablo Sinha

Russia says no peace dialogue ongoing with Japan
data.description:
MOSCOW, Feb 20 (Reuters) - The Kremlin said on Friday that relations with Japan had been reduced to zero over Tokyo's "hostile" stance towards Russia, and there was no ongoing dialogue towards peace.

Japanese Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi said in her inaugural address to parliament that Tokyo was keen on resolving its territorial issue with Russia and concluding a peace treaty.

Russia and Japan never signed a formal World War II peace treaty, with the main obstacle being an unresolved territorial dispute over the Kuril Islands, known in Japan as the Northern Territories.

(Reporting by Dmitry Antonov; Writing by Lucy Papachristou; Editing by Gleb Bryanski)

data.title: Global Markets Steady Ahead of Key U.S. Inflation Report
data.description:
By Dow Jones Newswires Staff


Global markets held steady ahead of key U.S. data that will help set the mood for the Federal Reserve's rate-cutting path this year. U.S. futures rose in early European trade after tech stocks slipped again Thursday, while oil prices held near their highest levels in around six months as the U.S. military buildup in the Middle East continued.

Markets will watch for U.S. PCE data--the Federal Reserve's preferred inflation gauge--due later Friday, as well as advance fourth-quarter economic growth data. The flash S&P purchasing managers' survey will also be released later Friday. The prints come after Fed officials, including Stephen Miran, struck an increasingly hawkish tone in recent days, helping lift the dollar and buoy Treasury yields as expectations of imminent rate cuts dimmed.

A long-awaited U.S. Supreme Court verdict on the legality of some of the tariffs levied by President Trump on other countries could also come Friday.


--Oil prices are on track for weekly gains of more than 5%, buoyed by rising concerns of a potential U.S. attack against Iran. President Trump said Iran has roughly 10 to 15 days to strike a deal. "Failure to do so would likely lead to U.S. military action against Iran," ING analysts said. "The key question is then how prolonged any action would be and what the ultimate aim of the U.S. is." In early trade, Brent crude and WTI were flat at $71.64 a barrel and $65.88 a barrel, respectively, holding near their highest levels in about six months. "The heightened uncertainty that the market faces over the next two weeks suggests that oil prices are likely to continue to price in a large risk premium," the analysts said.


--U.S. futures moved higher after a rocky day of trading Thursday. Futures tied to the Dow Jones Industrial Average climbed 0.2%, while the S&P 500 and Nasdaq both rose 0.3% premarket. Significant premarket movers included Alphabet, which gained 1.65%, and mobile technology company AppLovin, which gained 6%.


--Asian equity indexes were mixed Friday. The Korean Kospi continued its positive momentum, closing 2.3% higher as semiconductor memory-maker SK Hynix climbed a further 6.15%, bringing its year-to-date gain to over 45%. Japan's blue-chip Nikkei 225 slipped around 1.1% as consumer cyclicals fell. In Hong Kong, the Hang Seng index also fell 1.1% as online retailer Alibaba fell 4.9%. Markets in mainland China remained closed for the Lunar New Year holiday.


--European indexes largely gained at the open. Moncler surged 12.6% after reporting revenue ahead of estimates, driving luxury stocks across the continent higher and helping the FTSE MIB rise 0.4% in Milan. French luxuries bellwether LVMH rose 2.8%, spurring the CAC 40 0.5% higher. Fashion house Burberry led the FTSE 100's risers, as the U.K. blue-chip index gained 0.2%. The German DAX was flat, dragged by pharmaceutical group Bayer falling 1.8%. In Spain, the IBEX 35 fell 0.1% as a slip in the energy sector countered gains for industrials and healthcare stocks--including a 1.2% gain for ACS.


--The dollar traded steady against a basket of currencies ahead of U.S. data that could prompt markets to adjust interest-rate cut expectations. The DXY dollar index trades flat at 97.911, after hitting a four-week high of 98.074 Thursday.


--Ten-year Treasury yields were steady at 4.073%, having risen Thursday, Tradeweb data show. Treasurys are caught between risk-averse sentiment prompting safe-haven flows and concerns about the potential inflationary consequences of higher oil prices, Deutsche Bank analysts said in a note.

Yields on German government bonds fell and ING analysts said they could be favored over U.S. Treasurys amid worries about a possible U.S. strike on Iran. "Bunds might be preferred over U.S. Treasurys to hide from Iran-related turmoil," they said. Ten-year German Bund yields fell 1.1 bps to 2.732%.

In the U.K., government bond yields fell following better-than-expected public finances data. Ten-year gilt yields slid 2.7 basis points to last trade at 4.343%, Tradeweb data show.


--Bitcoin rose modestly as it showed some divergence from falls in U.S. stocks overnight, although its recovery remains tepid in the wake of its recent selloff. While bitcoin is slightly higher, it continues to trade below its key moving averages, UniCredit's Investment Institute strategist Thomas Strobel said in a note. "While sentiment remains fragile and downside risks persist, a potential recovery cannot be ruled out as broader conditions evolve." Bitcoin climbed 1.3% to $67,812. It has recovered since reaching a 16-month low of $60,008 on Feb.6, LSEG data showed.


--Gold prices held above the $5,000 mark, as a U.S. military buildup in the Middle East and fears of a potential strike against Iran lifted demand for safe-haven assets. In early trading, futures in New York rose 0.7% to $5,030.70 a troy ounce. "Prices have been volatile since a sharp correction from January's record highs, driven partly by speculative trading, but underlying support factors, including geopolitical risks and a shift away from traditional assets, remain in place," said Soojin Kim from MUFG.


Write to Barcelona Editors at barcelonaeditors@dowjones.com


(END) Dow Jones Newswires

02-20-26 0456ET

data.title: Dollar set for biggest weekly gain since October as Middle East tensions rise
data.description:
LONDON , Feb 20 (Reuters) - The dollar was on track for its largest weekly gain since October on Friday, buoyed by a run of better-than-expected economic data and a more hawkish Federal Reserve outlook, as tensions rose between the U.S. and Iran.

The dollar index, which measures the greenback against a basket of currencies, nudged slightly higher on Friday and was last set for a weekly rise of about 1.1% .

Jobs data lifted the dollar in the previous session, with the number of Americans filing new applications for unemployment benefits falling more than expected last week - underscoring labour market stability. 

Earlier in the week, minutes from the Fed's most recent meeting showed policymakers were divided on the outlook for rates, given the persistence of inflation.

"(Between) relatively solid data and a slightly less dovish Fed in terms of the minutes, some of this tension in the Middle East and ... a little bit of a positioning lining up, you can sort of see why the dollar's managed to bounce," said Dominic Bunning, head of G10 FX strategy at Nomura.

Investors often buy dollars when geopolitical tensions escalate.

MARKETS 'POSITIONING FOR RISK'

U.S. President Donald Trump warned Iran on Thursday it must make a deal over its nuclear programme or "really bad things" would happen, and set Tehran a deadline of 10 to 15 days to cooperate. Iran said it would retaliate against U.S. bases in the region if attacked.

"Given the build-up of military presence in the Middle East and the comments from Trump, I think markets will certainly be positioning for the risk of something happening over the weekend," said Derek Halpenny, MUFG's head of research - global markets EMEA.

A jump in crude oil prices could leave various currencies, including the euro, Japanese yen and British pound vulnerable, Halpenny said.

"Those are the currencies where you could get some bigger moves," he added.

Sterling last languished near a one-month low at $1.3455 and was heading for a weekly drop of 1.4%, its biggest since January 2025.

The euro was down 0.1% at $1.1760 and set to lose nearly 0.9% for the week, with the common currency also weighed down by uncertainty over European Central Bank President Christine Lagarde's tenure.

RATES, RATES, RATES

Markets on Friday also awaited the release of the U.S. core PCE price index and advance fourth quarter GDP figures later in the day, which could drive the next move in currencies.

Investors continue to price in roughly two Fed rate cuts this year, though expectations for such a move in June have dipped to a roughly 58% chance from 62% a week ago, according to the CME FedWatch tool.

"The big argument within the Fed is whether or not to proactively lower rates to support the job market, or to keep rates higher for longer in order to fight inflation," said Chris Zaccarelli, chief investment officer for Northlight Asset Management.

Friday's PCE report will "add to the debate", Zaccarelli added.

In Japan, data on Friday showed the country's annual core consumer inflation hit 2.0% in January, the slowest pace in two years.

"Today's data won't exactly instil a sense of urgency in the (Bank of Japan) to resume its tightening cycle, especially given the lacklustre rebound in activity last quarter," said Abhijit Surya, senior APAC economist at Capital Economics.

The yen fell more than 0.4% and was last at 155.53 per dollar.

The currency hardly reacted to a speech on Friday by Japanese Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi, who underlined her administration's commitment to revitalising the economy.

Elsewhere, the New Zealand dollar headed for a weekly fall of 1.3%, affected by a dovish outlook on rates from the Reserve Bank of New Zealand.

(Reporting by Sophie Kiderlin and Rae Wee;Editing by Shri Navaratnam, Kate Mayberry and Helen Popper)

By Sophie Kiderlin

data.title: S&P Global: Flash UK Composite PMI Rises to 22-month High in February

Anglo American Halves Value of De Beers Diamond Business -- 2nd Update
data.description:
By Adam Whittaker


Anglo American wrote down the value of its De Beers diamond business by $2.3 billion, the third cut in three years, as challenging market conditions complicate plans to dispose of the unit.

The London-listed miner warned earlier this month that it was undertaking a review of the business that could lead to the write-down. For 2025, the unit booked a roughly $500 million underlying earnings loss.

Friday's impairment halves the value of De Beers to $2.3 billion.

Soft economic growth in China, and the rise of cheaper lab-made diamonds has taken the shine off the storied unit. Diamond jewelry retailers are increasingly embracing synthetic diamonds, especially in products such as engagement rings.

In February of last year, Anglo wrote down De Beers's value by $2.9 billion, having already reduced it by $1.6 billion in 2024.

After fending off a roughly $50 billion hostile takeover bid from BHP in 2024, the miner has been simplifying its business by spinning off units and focusing on iron and copper. It agreed to merge with Canada's Teck Resources in September in a deal that places copper at the heart of the combined entity.

The rise of artificial diamonds has dented sales for De Beers, whose 1947 "A Diamond is Forever" marketing campaign is credited with enhancing the appeal of diamond rings. Weakness in the industrial diamond market has added to the unit's problems.

The company said Friday that it was progressing with the separation of De Beers. It is prioritizing a sale of the business and, given the continuing discussions with interested parties, Chief Executive Duncan Wanblad said he was optimistic a deal would be signed in 2026.

The impairment came as Anglo booked underlying earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation and amortization of $6.4 billion for last year compared with $6.3 billion in 2024. It booked a net loss of $3.7 billion, including the $2.3 billion De Beers pretax impairment.


Write to Adam Whittaker at adam.whittaker@wsj.com


(END) Dow Jones Newswires

02-20-26 0442ET

data.title: Anglo's painful makeover
data.description:
At first glance, Anglo American's core business is improving. Revenue from continuing operations rose to 18.546 billion dollars in 2025, up from $17.809bn the year before. Underlying EBITDA increased slightly to $6.417bn, from $6.322bn.

But once everything is included, businesses being sold and large one-off charges, the group still made a heavy loss. The total loss attributable to shareholders widened to $3.741bn.

A slimmer Anglo... On paper

For the past two years, management has argued that a smaller, more focused Anglo should be worth more. The idea is simple: fewer commodities, fewer countries, fewer complications.

In 2025, the “continuing” business made a profit before tax of $883m, compared with a loss of $1.364bn a year earlier. The improvement was helped by higher copper and iron ore prices, as well as cost-cutting and internal restructuring.

Cash generation was solid. Operating cash flow from continuing operations was $7.005bn, slightly higher than $6.930bn in 2024. Attributable free cash flow improved to an inflow of $0.79bn, compared with an outflow the year before. Lower capital spending and lower interest payments helped.

The balance sheet is also improving. Net debt fell to $8.571bn, down from $10.623bn, equal to 26% gearing (a measure of debt relative to total capital). Part of the reduction came from asset sales. Anglo received $2.432bn from selling its remaining stake in Valterra Platinum, as well as cash from the Jellinbah disposal.

The difficult parts: diamonds and disposals

The restructuring is not painless. De Beers, the diamond business, remains part of continuing operations and continues to struggle. It reported revenue of $3.5bn (up from $3.3bn), but made an underlying EBITDA loss of $511m.

Anglo also recorded a $2.3bn impairment at De Beers, reflecting lower expected diamond prices and growing competition from lab-grown stones.

Meanwhile, the businesses being sold - steelmaking coal, nickel and platinum group metals - weighed heavily on the overall result. Discontinued operations recorded a loss of $2.466bn, compared with a profit the previous year. The costs of separating and selling these assets are large and unpredictable.

There were also substantial one-off charges. In 2025, special items and remeasurements in continuing operations totalled $2.561bn. This shows that the gap between “underlying” profits and official reported results remains wide.

A reminder of mining's cycles

Dividends often show how confident a company feels. Anglo proposed a final dividend of 16 cents per share, down from 22 cents. The interim dividend was 7 cents, bringing the total for the year to 23 cents. In 2024, the interim dividend alone was 42 cents.

Management describes this as sticking to its payout policy. Others may see it as a reminder that mining remains cyclical, even after simplification.

There is some encouragement. Continuing operations generated operating profit of $1.352bn, compared with an operating loss a year earlier. The core business is stabilising.

The bigger question is whether Anglo can complete its reshaping successfully. Selling assets and recording impairments may simplify the company. But investors will ultimately judge it on whether its remaining businesses can deliver steady profits, rather than on how neatly the portfolio is rearranged.

data.title: Nimble Unit Emerson Radio's Nine-Month Loss Widens
SEB Proposes Cancellation of Repurchased Shares
data.description:
Shareholders of SEB bank are invited to the Annual General Meeting on March 24 in Stockholm. Shareholders may also vote in advance by post.

The Board of Directors proposes that the meeting approve a dividend of SEK 11 per share, consisting of SEK 8.50 in ordinary dividend and SEK 2.50 in extra dividend.

The board's proposal has been known for some time. A new appointment is proposed, with Martina Wallenberg nominated as a new board member.

Board member Winnie Fok has declined re-election.

Re-election is proposed for Chairman Marcus Wallenberg as well as board members Jacob Aarup-Andersen, Signhild Arnegård Hansen, Jan Erik Back, Anne-Catherine Berner, John Flint, Svein Tore Holsether, Eva Lindholm, Lars Ottersgård, and Johan Torgeby.

The meeting is also proposed to decide on an incentive program for 2026, targeting up to 1,000 participants and a maximum of 9.9 million shares.

The Board proposes that the meeting resolve to cancel 57.24 million own A-shares that have been repurchased.

Bajaj Finance Raises INR25 Billion Through Debenture Allotment
UBS lifts forecast for big tech bond sales this year
data.description:
(Refiles February 18 story to remove Reuters Instrument Codes for Oracle and CoreWeave. No changes to text)

LONDON, Feb 18 (Reuters) - UBS lifted its 2026 forecasts for U.S. tech investment grade bond sales on Wednesday, pointing to rising spending by big tech firms, while cutting its forecast for leveraged loans on expectations that AI-related disruption could curb supply.

Several megacap tech companies including Meta, Amazon and Alphabet have announced big increases to their capital expenditure plans during the latest earnings season.

After years of outsized gains, big tech stocks, meanwhile, have fallen in 2026, as investors question whether heavy AI spending will generate sufficient returns to warrant lofty valuations.

UBS's global credit team in a note raised its U.S. investment grade tech issuance forecast to $360 billion from $300 billion.

That takes UBS's overall forecast for U.S. investment grade debt issuance from $1.725 trillion to $1.8 trillion this year, with tech accounting for a fifth of that.

It also cut its U.S. leveraged loans forecast to $360 billion from $450 billion.

HYPERSCALER CAPEX TO OUTPACE BANK'S PREVIOUS FORECASTS

If recently announced capex increases are realised, UBS sees aggregate capex spending by so-called hyperscalers approaching $770 billion for 2026 - around 23% higher than the bank's previous expectations.

Hyperscaler public debt issuance, UBS said, could increase by an additional $40 billion to $50 billion to as much as $240 billion.

UBS also expects more non-U.S. dollar supply in the tech sector versus previous years. Last week, Alphabet tapped the sterling and Swiss franc markets as part of a $31.51 billion global bond raise.

"Alphabet's recent CHF (Swiss franc) and GBP (sterling) bond deals imply that U.S. tech companies will continue to look globally to fund capex," UBS analysts said in the note.

Late 2025 saw big tech firms shift to tapping debt markets to fund AI data centres, leading to a surge in issuance across a range of debt markets.

In recent weeks, concerns over how powerful AI models might disrupt traditional business models have also proliferated through markets.

UBS is lowering its leveraged loan issuance forecast on an expectation that disruption created by AI is most underpriced in leveraged loans (LL) and private credit markets. Potentially wider spreads in the LL space due to higher disruption risk could hit refinancing activity, says UBS.

(Reporting by Lucy Raitano; Editing by Dhara Ranasinghe and Joe Bavier)

By Lucy Raitano

data.title: Trump ally ties up with Russia's Novatek on natural gas in Alaska, NYT reports
data.description:
MOSCOW, Feb 20 (Reuters) - American financier Gentry Beach, who has ties to U.S. President Donald Trump's family, signed an agreement with Russia's energy giant Novatek last autumn to develop natural gas in Alaska amid Western sanctions against Russia, the New York Times reported on Friday.

In August, Trump and Russian President Vladimir Putin met in Alaska for talks aimed at ending Russia's war in Ukraine.

U.S. and Russian officials discussed several potential energy deals on the sidelines of the negotiations. Sources familiar with the talks said the business proposals were designed to encourage the Kremlin to agree to a peace deal in Ukraine and for Washington to ease sanctions on Russia.

The war is still raging in Ukraine after four years.

The New York Times, which spoke to the Texas financier, said he had quietly signed an agreement for Novatek to develop natural gas in Alaska.

He told the newspaper that the project was in its early stages and faced significant hurdles, declining to disclose the financial details.

Novatek told the newspaper it was "indeed having negotiations on the potential use" of its technology to liquefy natural gas in remote northern Alaska, but it did not confirm that it was working with Beach.

Novatek did not reply to a request for comment from Reuters. Beach was not immediately available for comment.

Beach is chairman and CEO of investment firm America First Global that holds interests in energy, mining and infrastructure. He helped raise funds for Trump's election campaign in 2016 and contributed to shaping  the administration's "America First" economic and diplomatic agenda.

Beach is also a college friend of Trump's son, Donald Trump Jr., according to the New York Times. 

(Reporting by Vladimir Soldatkin, editing by Andrei Khalip)

data.title: Flash PMI: Eurozone Private Sector Output Edges Up in February
Deutsche Börse News: "High Volumes in Tech Stocks" (Funds)
data.description:
FRANKFURT (DEUTSCHE-BOERSE AG) - Technology funds continue to dominate trading activity. Internationally oriented equity funds are more likely to be on the sales lists, while mixed funds are in demand. In precious metals, many investors are taking advantage of the high volatility for targeted entry and exit points.

February 20, 2026. FRANKFURT (Deutsche Börse). After a furious start to the year, activity in fund trading has now calmed somewhat. "In the past two weeks, business has slowed down a bit," reports Ivo Orlemann of ICF Bank. Until the beginning of February, there was considerably more going on. The main focus was on tech stocks. The trader observes "mostly purchases in this segment, even though there have been repeated redemptions."

+++

Online Session: February 23, 12 noon: European Corporate Bonds - Active ETFs and Index ETFs Compared. With Dr. Markus Thomas, Xenix #293

Actively managed ETFs versus classic index ETFs: How do ETF strategies differ for European corporate bonds, and which criteria are decisive for practical application?

Dr. Markus Thomas offers clear insights and analyzes selected euro-denominated corporate bond ETFs from the XENIX Rating Database in a direct comparison.

Register now for free: live.deutsche-boerse.com/webinare

+++

One possible reason for this, in his view, is uncertainty due to ongoing discussions about the concrete effects of the AI boom and the enormous investments in AI infrastructure. The current leader in turnover is the FF - Global Technology Fund AD (LU0099574567) from Fidelity, which is predominantly being bought. The managed volume has now risen to around 29 billion euros. Over the past ten years, the fund has gained 539 percent. Also heavily traded is the DWS Smart Industrial Technologies (DE0005152482), formerly known as DWS ZukunftsInvestitionen.

Currencies influence positioning

Anja Deisenroth-Boström of Baader Bank sees "high volatility in the market." Among investors, the trend is "more on the selling side, driven by currency movements." The strong euro has weighed on the performance of many funds in recent months. Recently, however, there has been some tailwind from the currency side. Especially being sold are globally investing equity funds such as the Morgan Stanley INVF Global Opportunity Fund (LU0552385295), the DWS Global Value (LU0133414606), and the DWS Akkumula (DE0008474024). There are also increased profit-taking moves in the DWS Top Dividende (DE0009848119), which just reached a new all-time high.

According to Deisenroth-Boström, European equities are primarily being ordered via the Alken Fund European Opportunities (LU0235308482). Mostly being sold are the Comgest Growth Europe AC (IE0004766675) and the cominvest Fondak P (DE0008471012), which focuses on German stocks. Multi-asset strategies such as ARERO - Der Weltfonds (LU0360863863) remain on the shopping lists of Baader Bank clients. Orlemann also confirms: "Mixed funds are always running." As examples, he names the FvS SICAV Multiple Opportunities (LU0323578657) and the FvS Multiple Opportunities II (LU0952573482).

Silver stocks: Buying after setbacks

In precious metals, many investors are still using the relatively high volatility for counter-cyclical entry and exit points. Orlemann reports particularly high demand for the STABILITAS - SILBER+WEISSMETALLE P (LU0265803667), which is preferably ordered "on weak days." Deisenroth-Boström also sees "mainly purchases" in this fund, although stop-losses are sometimes triggered. Here, investments are not made directly in raw materials, but in shares of companies that mine, process, and market silver and other "white" metals such as palladium or platinum.

Real estate funds: No lasting panic

In the much-discussed sector of open-ended real estate funds, turnover has dropped sharply according to Orlemann. "Despite negative news, there was no increased selling pressure." In mid-January, the Wohnselect Kapitalverwaltungsgesellschaft suspended redemptions of shares in the residential property-focused Wertgrund Wohnselect D (DE000A1CUAY0) with immediate effect, citing the generally high volume of redemptions. The last time such measures were taken was during the industry crisis from 2008 to 2012. The fund's price fell from just over 75 euros to 66 euros immediately after the announcement, but has since recovered to 71 euros. The fund has also been bought at this price level.

According to BVI, assets under management for the German fund market (including ETFs) reached a new record high of 4.85 trillion euros at the end of 2025. Of the net inflows totaling 86 billion euros last year, two-thirds went to ETFs and one-third to classic investment funds. The largest growth was recorded by equity funds with an increase of 26.7 percent and bond funds with a gain of 21.1 percent. In contrast, assets under management in real estate funds fell by 8.4 percent.

By Thomas Koch, February 19, 2026, © Deutsche Börse AG

(Deutsche Börse AG is solely responsible for the content of this column. The articles do not constitute an invitation to buy or sell securities or other assets.)
